summary: I arrived at Ellis Island on April 19, 1910, coming from Russia with my family. We traveled on the ship Furnessia, coming from Glasgow after leaving Odessa. Ellis Island was our gateway to America, where we went through Customs before starting our new life.

I was born in Elizabethgrad, Russia, near the Inhul River in 1908. Life there was difficult, especially for Jewish families like mine, because of the Pogroms and restrictions in the Pale of Settlement. My family decided to leave Russia to seek freedom and safety in America.

After arriving in America, my family first stayed at 57 East 98th Street in Manhattan. We lived there while adjusting to life in a new country and learning English, since we only spoke Yiddish. Later, we moved back to Manhattan after spending some time in New Haven, Connecticut, and I worked hard there as a bookkeeper.

I was born in Elizabethgrad, Russia, in 1908, near the Inhul River. It was a small town where my family lived before we left due to the dangerous Pogroms against Jewish people. Leaving Elizabethgrad was the start of our journey to America.

Elizabethgrad, my birthplace, is located near the Inhul River in Russia. This area was part of the Pale of Settlement where many Jewish families, including mine, lived before emigrating. The river was part of the landscape of my early childhood before we left for America.

Our family likely took a ship from Odessa to Glasgow on our way to America. Odessa was an important port city in Russia from which many Jewish families, including mine, departed to escape persecution. It was a crucial step in our long journey to Ellis Island.

After leaving Odessa, we traveled to Glasgow, Scotland, before boarding the Furnessia to America. Glasgow was a stopover on our difficult journey from Russia to Ellis Island. It was part of the route that brought us closer to our new life in the United States.

We traveled to America aboard the ship Furnessia, arriving at Ellis Island in 1910. My family and I were in the 4th Steerage, which was crowded and uncomfortable for such a long trip. Despite the hardships, the Furnessia brought us safely to a new beginning.

My family lived in the Pale of Settlement in Russia, an area where Jewish people were confined and faced many hardships. The Pogroms and anti-Jewish attacks there made life dangerous, so my parents decided to leave for America. The Pale of Settlement was the place we escaped from in search of freedom.

When we first arrived in America, my family only spoke Yiddish, which made adjusting to life here challenging. It took time to learn English and adapt to the new culture. Yiddish was the language of our home and community during those early years.

After spending some time in Manhattan, my family moved to New Haven, Connecticut to live with relatives. This helped us feel more comfortable as we adjusted to life in America. Eventually, we moved back to Manhattan once we were ready to be on our own.

I was determined to get an education despite my family's financial struggles. After dropping out to help support my family, I convinced my parents to let me return to school. I graduated from Julia Richmond High School, which was a proud moment for me.

My family eventually moved to the Bronx, where I worked as a bookkeeper because I was good with numbers. It was also where I met my future husband at a Halloween party when I was 21. We raised our family there and built our life together.

The Pogroms were violent anti-Jewish attacks in Russia that made life unbearable for my family. These attacks in the Pale of Settlement were a major reason my parents decided to leave Russia. Escaping the Pogroms meant we could finally live without fear in America.

The United States gave my family and me a chance to live freely and pursue our dreams. Though the transition was difficult, with language barriers and financial struggles, we persevered. America allowed me to work, get an education, and raise a family in a way that would have been impossible in Russia.
